coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive inner filter effect fluorescence sensing platform for alkaline phosphatase based on arginine surface-engineered gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Jincheng
Zhang, Shuai
Qin, Linqian
Shan, Wenchong
Sun, Boyan
Shen, Jianzhong
Jiang, Haiyang
coredata.dc:description: Developing a simple yet powerful method for enzyme activity monitoring is of great significance in the early diagnosis of diseases. Herein, we designed a precise spectral overlap-triggered inner filter effect (IFE) fluorescence sensing platform for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) detection. ALP could catalyze hydrolysis of p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) to generate p-nitrophenol (PNP) and PNP acts as a powerful acceptor in IFE reaction system to influence the excitation of fluorescent donors. Based on this principle, a standardized procedure was proposed for rational screening of high-performance donor/acceptor pairs to maximize IFE efficiency. The results proved that perfect spectral overlap and high quantum yield of fluorescent donors synergistically contribute to maximizing IFE efficiency. In this case, a high quantum yield of 51.7% of the arginine surface-engineered gold nanoclusters (Arg/ATT-AuNCs) produced perfect spectral overlap with PNP and achieved maximal IFE efficiency, which was eventually selected as fluorescent signal tag for ALP detection. The Arg/ATT-AuNCs-based approach achieved ultrasensitive detection of ALP with a detection limit of 0.0062 U/L, which exhibited 4.2–25.8-fold improvement compared to other donors. Furthermore, the accuracy and reliability of established method in real human serum samples were verified by spiking analysis and commercial kits, thus providing a promising platform for monitoring ALP. This work aims to provide a standardized procedure for rational screening high-performance donor/acceptor pairs and to develop an ultrasensitive IFE sensing platform for biomarkers detection, which will open up a promising horizon for other biomarkers detection in clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inner filter effect
Alkaline phosphatase
Rational screening
Donor/acceptor pairs, Gold nanoclusters
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of key factors that affect IFE efficiency among fluorescent donors
Detecting ALP using IFE-based sensing platform
Fabrication and characterization of arginine surface-engineered AuNCs
Evaluation of IFE efficiency to screen high-performance donor/acceptor pairs
Construction of fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection
Analytical performances of fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection
Determination of ALP activity in human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Beijing Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 6222021 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of Arg/ATT-AuNCs. (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Arg/ATT-AuNCs. (b) UV-Vis and fluorescence spectrum. Inset image: photograph of AuNCs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (c) High-resolution TEM image. Inset image: size distribution histogram calculated from 100 nanoparticles. (d) DLS distributions. (e) XPS image and their Au4f (f), S2p (g) spectrum.

IFE efficiency evaluation at their respective maximal excitation wavelength. (a) Screening procedure of optimal donor/acceptor pairs. (b-f) The left part was the schematic illustration for ALP detection using different fluorescent donors; the middle part shows the overlap between the absorption of PNP (pink lines) and the excitation of various fluorescent donors (blue lines); the right part shows the corresponding QE of various donors after ALP enzymatic reaction. (1) normalized intensity of fluorescent donors containing 0.5 mM PNPP and 0.1 μM Mg2+, fluorescence QE in the presence of 1 U/L (2) and 10 U/L (3) after ALP enzymatic reaction.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PNP and PNPP and ﬂuorescence excitation and emission spectrum of Arg/ATT-AuNCs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectrum Arg/ATT-AuNCs in the presence of various levels of PNP from 0 to 500 μM. (c) The linearity of F/F0 versus PNP concentration. F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of Arg/ATT-AuNCs in the absence and presence of PNP, respectively. (d) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of PNPP in the presence of different amounts of ALP from 0 to 50 U/L. The inset photograph showed the enzymatic reaction solutions with various levels of ALP activity. (e) The linearity of A-A0 versus ALP activity. A and A0 were the absorbance of PNP in the presence and absence of ALP, respectively. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of Arg/ATT-AuNCs, Arg/ATT-AuNCs/PNP (100 μM), Arg/ATT-AuNCs /PNPP (0.5 mM), and Arg/ATT-AuNCs/PNPP/ALP (1 and 10 U/L) system.

(a) Schematic illustration IFE-based fluorescence sensing platform for ALP activity. (b) Fluorescence emission spectrum of AuNCs/PNPP reaction system in the presence of various activities of ALP. Inset image was fluorescence response under UV light. (c) Linear relationship of F/F0 versus ALP activity. F and F0 were fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of ALP in reaction system, respectively. (d) Selectivity of proposed sensor against the other seven enzymes, all enzyme activities were 10 U/L. (e) Selectivity of proposed sensor against other disturbing substances. The metal ions, amino acids, and biomolecules were all 100 μM, and proteins were 100 mg/L. (f) Fluorescence emission spectrum of AuNCs/PNPP/ALP reaction system in the presence of various concentrations of Na3VO4 (0–1000 μM). (g) Plots and linear range of inhibition ratio versus inhibitor concentration. The reaction system contained various concentrations of inhibitors, 0.5 mM PNPP, 10 U/L ALP and 0.1 μM Mg2+.
Comparison analysis (a) and correlation relationship (b) of detection results between proposed assay and commercial kits among eight human serum samples.
Comparison of detection performances of other reported IFE-based sensors for ALP activity detection.
Spiking analysis of proposed assay in eight human serum samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: a determined by commercial kits. b the amount of ALP (44 U/L) added to the actual serum sample. c the total amount detected after adding 44 U/L ALP to the actual serum sample. d Recovery%= (total detected -reference values)/spiked × 100%. e the relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive inner filter effect fluorescence sensing platform for alkaline phosphatase based on arginine surface-engineered gold nanoclusters

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – original draft
Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing
Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Veterinary Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Veterinary Medicine, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety, Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety, Beijing 100193, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Veterinary Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Veterinary Medicine, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety, Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Veterinary Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Veterinary Medicine, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety, Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety, Beijing 100193, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a simple yet powerful method for enzyme activity monitoring is of great significance in the early diagnosis of diseases. Herein, we designed a precise spectral overlap-triggered inner filter effect (IFE) fluorescence sensing platform for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) detection. ALP could catalyze hydrolysis of p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) to generate p-nitrophenol (PNP) and PNP acts as a powerful acceptor in IFE reaction system to influence the excitation of fluorescent donors. Based on this principle, a standardized procedure was proposed for rational screening of high-performance donor/acceptor pairs to maximize IFE efficiency. The results proved that perfect spectral overlap and high quantum yield of fluorescent donors synergistically contribute to maximizing IFE efficiency. In this case, a high quantum yield of 51.7% of the arginine surface-engineered gold nanoclusters (Arg/ATT-AuNCs) produced perfect spectral overlap with PNP and achieved maximal IFE efficiency, which was eventually selected as fluorescent signal tag for ALP detection. The Arg/ATT-AuNCs-based approach achieved ultrasensitive detection of ALP with a detection limit of 0.0062 U/L, which exhibited 4.2–25.8-fold improvement compared to other donors. Furthermore, the accuracy and reliability of established method in real human serum samples were verified by spiking analysis and commercial kits, thus providing a promising platform for monitoring ALP. This work aims to provide a standardized procedure for rational screening high-performance donor/acceptor pairs and to develop an ultrasensitive IFE sensing platform for biomarkers detection, which will open up a promising horizon for other biomarkers detection in clinical diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomarkers are characteristic indicators that can reflect common physiological, pathological or therapeutic processes of human health. Detection of specific biomarkers is essential for disease identification, early diagnosis and monitoring during prevention and treatment[1,2]. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is extensively distributed in liver, bone, intestine, kidney and placenta of human body, which acts as a hydrolase that can catalyze hydrolysis of acids, proteins, and some small molecules[3]. Abnormal expression levels of ALP in human serum are thought to be associated with a variety of diseases, such as breast cancer, prostate cancer, bone disease, diabetes, and liver dysfunction[4–6]. Therefore, the development of simple and highly sensitive analytical techniques to monitor ALP activity is critical for the clinical diagnosis of various diseases. To date, numerous approaches have been developed for the determination of ALP levels in various types of samples, including colorimetry[7] electrochemistry[8], chemiluminescence[9], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS)[10]. Even though these methods possess their characteristics, there are inevitably suffered from drawbacks of poor sensitivity, expensive instruments and complicated operations. Thus, new analysis methods characterized by the merits of high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, easy operation, and low cost for ALP detection are urgently demanded.

Fluorescent sensors have been widely applied in the clinical diagnosis field due to their simple operation, cost-effectiveness, low background and quick responsiveness, favorable sensitivity and selectivity[11]. Several fluorescent materials have been utilized as sensing probes for ALP activity detection such as quantum dots[12–14], carbon dots[15,16], silicon nanoparticles[17], noble metal nanoclusters[18]. Among these materials, fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have attracted extensive attention in biosensing due to their simple synthesis, good water solubility and biocompatibility, abundant functional groups, and large Stokes shift[19]. Unfortunately, existing fluorescent AuNCs sensors usually are not sensitive enough due to the low quantum yield (QY<10%). Inspiringly, Deng’s team recently prepared arginine (Arg) surface-engineered light-emitting enhanced AuNCs with a surprising 65% QY through a supramolecular host-guest recognition between arginine and 6-aza-2-thiothymine (ATT) employing a ligand-rigid strategy [20]. As a powerful light-emitting material, our team has actively explored its application in the field of sensing and established various fluorescent and colorimetric/fluorescent immunosensors and aptasensor to detect β-adrenergic agonists, pyrrolizidine alkaloids, antibiotics, antiviral drugs and mycotoxins with satisfied analysis performance [21–24]. Based on the abovementioned foundation of research, our motivation in this work is to try to establish a clinical diagnostic fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection using Arg/ATT-AuNCs as signal probes.

The current sensing mechanism of fluorescent sensors mainly involves fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoelectron transfer (PET), electron energy transfer (EET) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). The design of fluorescent sensors based on the above mechanisms usually requires a specific distance between donor and acceptor for the electron or energy transfer process, complex operations and harsh test conditions may limit their further applications [25]. In contrast, inner filter effect (IFE) requires only that the absorption of the acceptor overlap with the excitation/emission of the donor, or both, to convert changes in absorption values into changes in fluorescence intensity[26]. Meanwhile, no surface modification of donor/acceptor pair is required to meet the specific distance to make the fluorescence analysis easier and more sensitive. Theoretically, the key to maximizing IFE efficiency is to expand the spectral overlap between absorption of the acceptor and excitation/emission of the donor[26,27]. Recent research has found that the inherent luminescent characteristics of fluorescent donors also contribute positively to IFE efficiency[28]. However, it is regrettable that few studies have focused on how the intrinsic properties of the acceptor/donor pair affect its IFE efficiency and the corresponding detection performance. Therefore, it is necessary to propose a standardized procedure for rational screening high-performance donor/acceptor pairs to maximize IFE efficiency before constructing IFE-based fluorescent sensors.

Herein, we proposed a standardized procedure for rational screening of high-performance donor/acceptor pairs and construction of an ultrasensitive ALP sensing platform using Arg surface-engineered light-emitting enhanced AuNCs as a fluorescent indicator. Firstly, a standardized procedure for the systematic comparative investigation of relationship between donor/acceptor pairs and their IFE efficiency was proposed. ALP could catalyze the hydrolysis of substrate p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) to produce yellow product p-nitrophenol (PNP) at the maximal absorption peak of 405 nm, which exhibited a favorable overlap with the excitation of donors, thus producing IFE and quenching the fluorescence of donors. The results demonstrated that perfect spectral overlap and high QY of fluorescent donors synergistically contributed to maximizing IFE efficiency. The highly luminescent green-emitting Arg/ATT-AuNCs with an absolute QY of 51.7% produced perfect spectral overlap with PNP and were eventually selected as fluorescent donor for construction of an IFE-based sensing platform for ALP detection. The proposed sensor achieved ultrasensitive detection of ALP with favorable anti-interference capability. Finally, the practicability and reliability of the proposed platform were verified by ALP inhibitors screening and real human serum sample determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of key factors that affect IFE efficiency among fluorescent donors
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand how these factors affected their IFE efficiency in fluorescence sensing, we proposed a standardized procedure for screening optimal fluorescent donor among six fluorescent donors. The donors including 3-MPA/BSA@AuNCs, BSA@AuNCs, Arg/ATT-AuNCs, GSH@AuNCs, Met@AuNCs, and CdSe/ZnS QDs with distinct characteristics were used for IFE efficiency evaluation. The synthesis methods of materials in detail were provided in supporting information. The evaluation procedure was described as follows, 10 μL of ALP solution at 0, 1, and 10 U/L were added into 0.01 mM Tris-HCl (pH=9.0) reaction system containing 0.5 mM PNPP and 0.1 μM Mg2+, and fluorescence intensity were standardized by adjusting the concentration of fluorescent donors to achieve the same level in reaction system. The fluorescence intensity was measured at the end of reaction and calculated their fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting ALP using IFE-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The following procedures were employed for ALP detection. A total volume of 10 μL ALP solution was added into 0.01 mM Tris-HCl (pH=10.0) reaction system containing certain concentration of fluorescent donor, 0.5 mM PNPP, 0.1 μM Mg2+. The reaction solution was placed in a 37 ℃ incubator for 1 h. The reaction solution was transferred to fluorescence plate for recording fluorescence intensity at their respective maximal excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of arginine surface-engineered AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The Arg surface-engineered light-emitting enhanced AuNCs were prepared according to previous report as illustrated in Fig. 1 a [20]. The efficient radiation-free relaxation of ATT molecules leading weak fluorescence of ATT-AuNCs. after additions of Arg, a strong host-guest interaction occurred between ATT and Arg, greatly suppressing the intramolecular vibrations and rotations of ATT and drastically increasing the luminescence efficiency. We utilized UV-Vis spectrometry, fluorescence spectrometry, TEM, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for full characterizations of Arg/ATT-AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 1 b, the Arg/ATT-AuNCs solution was yellow-green with a maximal absorption wavelength at 405 nm. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Arg/ATT-AuNCs were 405 and 528 nm, respectively, and its' stokes shift reached 123 nm width. The highly symmetrical fluorescence emission spectrum and narrow half-peak emission width (about 30 nm) indicated that Arg/ATT-AuNCs have good particle size homogeneity[29]. The initially prepared ATT-AuNCs exhibited only weak fluorescence with an absolute QY of 1.5%, and showed rather poor stability due to open binding sites of its surface structure[20]. The QY was dramatically increased to 51.7% after the addition of Arg, an approximately 35-fold improvement compared with ATT-AuNCs (Fig. S1). This could be interpreted as guanidine groups of Arg connected with ATT to form a rigid shell structure based on hydrogen bonding. The H-bond network greatly suppressed the intramolecular vibration and rotation of ATT, reducing nonradiative transition and energy loss, thus resulting in a significant increase in QY[30]. The superior anti-photobleaching capability of AuNCs was also demonstrated via a continuous 1 h of irradiation with an interval of 5 s, no significant changes in fluorescence intensity were observed during this process (Fig. S2). The zeta potential of Arg/ATT-AuNCs was − 40.7 mV confirming their favorable colloid stability (Fig. S3). TEM imaging of AuNCs showed spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.4 ± 0.2 nm from a count of 100 nanoparticles (Fig. 1 c). The DLS results of AuNCs reached an average diameter of 9.63 nm (Fig. 1 d), and the differences between TEM and DLS could be attributed to the outer layer of Arg molecules increased the thickness of nanoparticles in aqueous solution[20]. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the characteristic element peak of C, N, Au, S, and O appeared in the corresponding position of AuNCs XPS spectrum. Besides, the two Au 4 f peaks were located at the positions 84.0 eV (Au4f7/2) and 88.0 eV (Au4f7/2), respectively (Fig. 1 f). The Au-S and oxidized S were located at 162. 9 eV and 168.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 1 g). The XPS results were consistent with our previous reports[23,24]. In addition, AuNCs were also shown to have favorable stability (Fig. S4 and S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of IFE efficiency to screen high-performance donor/acceptor pairs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the occurrence of IFE has nothing to do with the distance between donor and receptor, improving the sensitivity of IFE-based sensing systems is thought to rely entirely on spectral overlap. In fact, several other factors such as QY of donor and molar absorption coefficient of acceptor also have positive impact on IFE efficiency. To better understand how these factors affect their IFE efficiency in fluorescence sensing platforms, herein, a standardized screening procedure was designed to explore how the inherent characteristics of donors affected their IFE efficiency and corresponding differences in detection performance ( Fig. 2 a). ALP could catalyze the hydrolysis of substrate PNPP to generate yellow product PNP with a maximal absorption wavelength of 405 nm, which could produce overlap with the excitation of the most fluorescent donors and influence their emission intensity. The six types of fluorescent donors including 3-MPA/BSA@AuNCs, BSA@AuNCs, Arg/ATT-AuNCs, GSH@AuNCs, Met@AuNCs, and CdSe/ZnS QDs with distinct excitation/emission wavelength, QY, and surface ligand were selected for this investigation, the detailed information about these fluorescent donors was illustrated in Fig. S6 -10. These excitation wavelengths of fluorescent donors ranged from 345 to 425 nm, showing varied extents of spectral overlap with PNP absorption.

To standardize the evaluation procedure of IFE efficiency, the fluorescence intensity of each fluorescent donor was adjusted to the same level in the presence of 0.5 mM PNPP. As shown in Fig. 2 b-g, in the presence of ALP activity of 1 and 10 U/L, the fluorescence of five donors was considerably quenched except for 3-MPA/BSA@AuNCs The acidic environment provided by the surface ligand MPA greatly inhibited ALP activity, while few spectral overlap produced between PNP and excitation of MPA/BSA@AuNCs[31]. The superposition of these two factors made MPA/BSA@AuNCs produce negligible fluorescence quenching in the presence of ALP. The other five fluorescent donors exhibited varying degrees of fluorescence quenching. The Arg/ATT-AuNCs achieved the highest fluorescence QE compared to other AuNCs. A remarkable feature here was that the distance between the maximum excitation wavelength of the donors and the PNP absorption peak directly affected the extent of spectral overlap, thus exhibiting observable regular differences in fluorescence QE. Besides, a green-emitting commercial CdSe/ZnS QDs with 70% QY also achieved comparable QE with Arg/ATT-AuNCs, although not showed precise spectral overlap with PNP, the high QY allowed the dilution ratio of CdSe/ZnS QDs reached a staggering 80,000. This enabled PNP molecules to better modulate the fluorescence of individual nanoparticles, reducing the signal transduction resistance between the donor/acceptor, thereby improving the QE. Furthermore, we noticed that QE of CdSe/ZnS QDs was 7.1 and 2.2 times higher than Met@AuNCs in the presence of 1 and 10 U/L of ALP, respectively. Even though they exhibited similar excitation wavelength, the large gap in QY was responsible for this phenomenon, which reflected that based on spectral overlap, the QY of the fluorescent donor was also an important driver of the increased IFE efficiency. The specific parameters were listed in Table S1. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. S11, the substrate PNPP produced obvious quenching of 3-MPA/BSA@AuNCs and BSA@AuNCs, but had no significant effect on the other four donors in the presence of 0.5 mM PNPP. The results showed that the selection of donors that avoid fluorescence quenching due to substrate interference was conducive to improving accuracy of IFE sensing platform. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength of five donors was set at 405 nm to achieve maximum spectral overlap with PNP to explore how much improvement in QE was achieved at the theoretically optimal spectral overlap compared to their respective maximal excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. S12, at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm, the QE of BSA@AuNCs exhibited 3.2 and 2-fold improvements in the presence of 1 and 10 U/L ALP activity. The GSH@AuNCs and Met@AuNCs exhibited 1.2 and 1.9-fold improvements in the presence of 1 U/L ALP activity, respectively. These results demonstrated that the incremental spectral overlap was more effective in converting absorbance of acceptor into fluorescence intensity changes of donors. The QE of CdSe/ZnS QDs did not show significant improvement, and the reason for this was that the usage of higher concentrations of CdSe/ZnS QDs reduced the enhancement effect of extent of spectral overlap on the QE. Arg/ATT-AuNCs still achieved the best QE compared to the other four donors. The specific parameters were listed in Table S2. These results demonstrated that precise spectral overlap produced between excitation of donors and absorption of acceptors was the primary factor in the development of an IFE-based sensing platform for ALP detection, which was the key to improving QE. While the selection of fluorescent donors with high QY was also very important to improve the fluorescence response of low-concentration analytes so that the QE could be maximized thereby improving the sensitivity during the analysis process. Therefore, after careful IFE efficiency evaluation, Arg/ATT-AuNCs were considered the best fluorescent donor for the subsequent construction of ALP sensing platform.

In this work, experiencing a standardized screening of donor/acceptor pairs with IFE efficiency assessment, the Arg surface-engineered light-emitting enhanced AuNCs were selected as optimal fluorescence donor with PNP[20]. The superiorities of using Arg/ATT-AuNCs as fluorescent donor for the construction of ultrasensitive IFE sensing platform for ALP detection could be summarized as follows: (i) the excitation of AuNCs produced perfect overlap with absorption of PNP and achieved maximal IFE efficiency compared to other five donors. (ii) the Stokes shift of AuNCs reached 123 nm width which can avoid the possible fluorescence quenching caused by PNPP. (iii) facile synthesis, favorable stability, excellent luminescence and high QY (51.7%) of AuNCs provide an eco-friendly and low-cost signal tag in biosensing platform. These excellent characteristics make it an ideal fluorescent donor and offer great scope for advancement in the design of diversified IFE biosensing platforms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a, after the ALP catalyzed hydrolysis of PNPP to produce PNP, with the maximum absorption wavelength of PNPP shifting from 312 nm to 405 nm of PNP, which displayed perfect spectral overlap with the maximal excitation peak of AuNCs, revealing that the sensing mechanism between AuNCs and PNP may be IFE. As illustrated in Fig. 3 b, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs gradually decreased with the increase of PNP concentration ranging from 1 to 500 μM, and the maximal emission wavelength of AuNCs also did not change. A good linear relationship was observed between PNP concentration versus QE F/F0 in the range of 1–25 μM with a regression equation of y = −0.018x+ 0.971 (R2 =0.936) (Fig. 3 c), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 μM (S/N = 3). To better visualize the process of ALP catalyzing the production of the yellow product PNP from the substrate PNPP, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum was used to record this dynamic process (Fig. 3 d). The absorption peak of PNPP gradually declined and the absorption peak of PNP gradually increased accompanied by the increase in ALP activity. Correspondingly, the reaction solution also changed from colorless to yellow due to the conversion of PNPP into PNP by ALP (insert image of Fig. 3 d). As depicted in Fig. 3 e, the regression equation of ALP activity versus A-A0 was presented as y = 0.072x+ 0.032 (R2 = 0.993) with a quantitative range of 0.5–10 U/L, and LOD was calculated to 0.32 U/L. The sensitivity of colorimetric method for ALP detection is usually lower than that of fluorescence assays, the transformation of color signal into fluorescence signal through IFE was an ideal strategy to improve detection sensitivity [11]. To prove the feasibility of IFE strategy used in this work, the effect of distinct components in reaction system on the fluorescence of AuNCs was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 f, the substrate PNPP alone produced a negligible effect on fluorescence intensity of AuNCs, while in contrast PNP caused an obvious decrease in fluorescence. When AuNCs, PNPP and ALP coexisted in the reaction system, the fluorescence intensity appeared to have a different degree of decline at 1, and 10 U/L of ALP activity. This is because the absorption peak of PNPP at 312 nm had no spectral overlap with the excitation of AuNCs, after ALP catalyzed hydrolysis PNPP to generate PNP, the absorption peak of PNP at 405 nm completely overlapped with the excitation of AuNCs, leading to fluorescence quenching through IFE, these results demonstrated the AuNCs could be used for construction of IFE-based fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection.

To further clarify the sensing principle in this work, more characterizations were conducted to ascertain the mechanism in fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by as-proposed PNP. Generally, the IFE process does not involve electron energy transfer and fluorescence lifetime change. The fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs alone and in the presence of PNP, PNPP, and PNPP+ALP (10 U/L) were investigated (Fig. S13a). The fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs, AuNCs+PNP, AuNCs+PNPP, AuNCs+PNPP+ALP (10 U/L) were 32.63, 34.60, 32.77, and 33.37 ns, respectively. No significant changes in fluorescence lifetime were observed, thus the dynamic quenching mechanism (FRET and PET) was excluded in AuNCs/PNP sensing system[32]. Meanwhile, we measured the UV-Vis absorption spectrum AuNCs, PNP, and their mixture as depicted in Fig. S13b , the absorption of the mixture did not shift compared to PNP, demonstrating there no strong interaction produced and formation of a ground-state complex between the AuNCs and PNP and excluded static quenching mechanism[33]. Moreover, the zeta potential of PNP was measured to explore whether it can interact electrostatically with AuNCs (Fig. S13c). The AuNCs and PNP were both negatively charged with zeta potentials of − 40.7 and − 26.1 mV, respectively, demonstrating impossible to generate FRET by bringing the distance closer via electrostatic interactions. Thus, the sensing mechanism in the present work is IFE, which was also consistent with previous reports[15,28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of fluorescence sensing platform for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a illustrated the principles of AuNCs-based sensing platform for ALP detection. Under the Optimal conditions (Fig. S14), the fluorescence response to different concentrations of ALP was measured. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased in presence of different activities of ALP in the range of 0–40 U/L. Fig. 4 c showed that the F/F0 (F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of Arg/ATT-AuNCs in the presence and absence of ALP in reaction system, respectively.) versus ALP activities present a good linear range between 0.02 and 1 U/L. The linear regression equations were y = −0.50x + 0.95 (R2 =0.992) and LOD of 0.0062 U/L (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), the LOD was 4.2, 5.2, 21.0 and 25.8 times lower than CdSe/ZnS QDs (Fig. S15a -b), GSH@AuNCs (Fig. S15c -d), BSA@AuNCs (Fig. S15e -f), and Met@AuNCs (Fig. S15g -h), respectively. These results indicated that the extent of spectral overlap was directly related to the sensitivity of the IFE-based sensing system, and high QY of donor was also an important driver for improving sensitivity. The developed fluorescence methods all achieved higher sensitivity compared to colorimetric method (0.32 U/L). The normal range of ALP in human serum ranged from 40 to 190 U/L[34]. The result provided herein is perfectly adequate for routine screening of ALP in clinical diagnosis.

Furthermore, the recently published IFE-based fluorescent sensors for ALP detection were selected for comparison of detection performance with our established method. As shown in Table 1, the proposed method achieved ultrasensitive detection of ALP, which is superior or comparable to current excellent performing IFE-based sensors for ALP activity. The arginine surface-engineered AuNCs with merits of being highly photoluminescent, environmentally friendly and cost-effective provide an affordable fluorescence tag for construction of in-vitro diagnostics fluorescent sensors in resource-poor areas. More importantly, the perfect spectral overlap and high QY of AuNCs synergistically contribute to maximizing IFE efficiency, and further increasing detection performances to a satisfactory level. As we know, this work is the first report using Arg/ATT-AuNCs/PNP as donor/acceptor pair for the development of an ultrasensitive IFE sensing platform for ALP detection.

The selectivity of proposed AuNCs-based IFE sensing platform was evaluated by testing the enzymes and other interfering substances. The seven enzymes (HRP, GOx, Ure, Lys, Tyr, CAT and Try) were added to the sample at an activity of 10 U/L and no obvious fluorescence changes were observed after introduction of these enzymes (Fig. 4 d). Moreover, other disturbing substances including metal ions (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+), negative ions (CH3COO-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, HCO3 -, CO3 2-), amino acids (tryptophan, methionine, arginine, threonine, serine, glycine, proline), biomolecules (ascorbic acid, glucose) and proteins (ovalbumin (OVA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), casein (CAS)) were tested and results showed in Fig. 4 e. Only the introduction of ALP led to a significant fluorescence decrease in the reaction system, while other co-existing substances produced negligible effect on fluorescence. It was noting worth that a significant fluorescence enhancement was observed with the addition of Arg, OVA, BSA, HAS, and CAS. Considering that serum contains a large number of proteins, amino acids, and other biomolecules, this interference needed to be eliminated before being applied for serum sample detection. Herein, we adopted a protein coupling strategy to enhance the stability of AuNCs. The proteins corona formation on the surface of the AuNCs greatly suppressed the ligand-related nonradiative relaxation pathway (intramolecular vibration and rotation) and led to prominent fluorescence enhancement and anti-interference properties[23,35]. The BSA was conjugated with AuNCs through carbodiimide methods to prepare AuNCs@BSA, its characterizations confirmed its successful coupling and were shown in Fig. S16. Compared to AuNCs, an approximately 2-fold improvement in fluorescence enhancement was observed (Fig. S17). Not only that, the interference of above five molecules on AuNCs@BSA was successfully eliminated (Fig. S18). The results indicated that application of protein coupling strategy significantly improved the stability and anti-interference ability of AuNCs, laying good foundation for the subsequent sensitive and selective determination of ALP in real human serum samples.

To better evaluate the applicability of proposed sensing platform, the enzyme inhibitor assay was used for assessing its suitability in practical applications. Na3VO4, as a common enzyme inhibitor was utilized for inhibition efficiency evaluation. The addition of Na3VO4 inhibited the activity of ALP, resulting in the inhibition of PNPP-catalyzed hydrolysis to generate PNP and IFE efficiency was correspondingly reduced. As shown in Fig. 4 f, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increasing concentration of inhibitor and kept stable at the Na3VO4 concentration of 500 μM. The inhibition ability was expressed by following equation, I (%) = ((F1-F0)/(FB-F0)) * 100%, where F1, FB and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity containing both ALP and inhibitor, none of ALP and inhibitor, ALP alone, respectively. The regression equation was fitted as y = 0.769x+ 3.77 (R2 =0.985) with a quantitative range of 1–100 μM, and LOD was 0.73 μM for Na3VO4 (Fig. 4 g). This result indicated the established method has satisfied sensitivity and could be applied for screening ALP relevant inhibitors in drug discovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALP activity in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical reliability of proposed sensing platform in real human serum samples, the developed approach was validated using spiking analysis and commercial kits. We first evaluated effect of serum ratio on fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and AuNCs@BSA, and the results showed that human serum rarely interfered with the fluorescence property of AuNCs@BSA (Fig. S19). However, the proposed method for ALP determination failed in the initial attempt. Similar results have been observed in several previous works[28,36]. The reason for this was that the ALP activity differed between the sources of different species. Therefore, the ALP activity needed to be corrected before the determination, and we used a commercial ALP kit to calculate the difference in ALP activity between calf intestinal mucosa and human serum to obtain calibration curve with a correction factor of 2.20 (Fig. S20). After successful calibration, 8 human clinical serum samples gifted from Beijing Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Beijing, China) were successfully measured using our proposed approach. As shown in Table 2, the recovery ranged from 93.6% to 107.3%, with relative standard deviation (RSD) all lower than 17.6%. Subsequently, the proposed method was directly used to detect ALP activity and compared with commercial kits. The results in Fig. 5 a indicated our proposed method achieved satisfactory consistency with commercial ALP kits. The correlation relationship of both methods was calculated and depicted in Fig. 5 b, a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.934 indicated that our proposed assay has good accuracy and reliability in detection of real human serum samples.

In summary, we successfully constructed an IFE fluorescence sensing platform based on Arg surfaced-engineered light-emitting enhanced AuNCs for simple, selective and ultrasensitive detection of ALP in human serum. A standardized screening procedure was first proposed to systemically explore the relationship between donor/acceptor pairs and IFE efficiency. The results demonstrated that perfect spectral overlap and high QY of fluorescent donors synergistically contribute to maximizing IFE efficiency. Arg/ATT-AuNCs integrated various advantages of facile preparation, excellent photoluminescence, good stability and dispersity and low cost, producing perfect spectral overlap with PNP to achieve the maximal IFE efficiency and were eventually selected as optimal fluorescence donor. The proposed approach achieved ultrasensitive detection of ALP with a LOD of 0.0062 U/L. Clinical serum samples from eight healthy volunteers were collected and evaluated using established approach. The obtained results were consistent with reference values determined by commercial ALP kits, demonstrating the clinical feasibility of this diagnostic technique for ALP monitoring. To the best of our knowledge, this work first proposed a standardized procedure for rational screening high-performance donor/acceptor pairs toward ALP detection, which will greatly help us to better understand the role of donor/acceptor pairs in IFE sensing platform. The proposed strategy is a positive and beneficial attempt, which will further expand the application of Arg/ATT-AuNCs as a powerful light-emitting probe in the field of biosensing.

Jincheng Xiong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Shuai Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Linqian Qin: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Wenchong Shan: Data curation, Formal analysis. Boyan Sun: Writing – review & editing. Jianzhong Shen: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Haiyang Jiang: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 6222021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.133177.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jincheng Xiong obtained master degree in Huazhong Agricultural University in 2019. He is currently a Ph.D. student under the guidance of Prof. Haiyang Jiang at China Agricultural University in China. His research interests are nanomaterials synthesis and applications for rapid detection of small molecules and biomarkers.

Shuai Zhang obtained master degree in Beijing University of Agriculture in 2017. His research interests focus on the development of lateral flow assay-based biosensors for rapid detection foodborne bacteria.
Linqian Qin obtained bachelor's degree in Northeast Agricultural University in 2021. Her research interests focus on the development of nanozymes-based immunoassay for small molecules detection.
Wenchong Shan obtained Ph.D. from China Agricultural University in 2022. His research interests focus on functional nucleic acid biosensing for small molecules.

Boyan Sun obtained bachelor's degree in Nanjing Agricultural University in 2022. His research interests are nanoparticles and nanoclusters synthesis and their applications in biosensing for biomarkers.

Jianzhong Shen is an academician of the Chinese Academy of Engineering and is currently a professor at the China Agricultural University. His main research interests are in veterinary drug residue detection technology, bacterial drug resistance and novel veterinary drug formulation development.

Haiyang Jiang obtained Ph.D. from China Agricultural University and began to work as a faculty in College of Veterinary Medicine of China Agricultural University. He is currently a professor in basic veterinary science. His research interests focus on advanced analytical techniques and the health effects of chemicals.
